title: Cold-Start Geometry

content.statement: The shape of your initial market determines your ability to start.
content.question: Is your market launch narrow enough? Should you start with one neighborhood, not the whole city?
sop: Define your current launch market, geography, niche, or customer type, as precisely as possible.
Rate its Density 1-10, how concentrated your target users are within that market (see calculation, page 6).
Rate its Niche Focus and Tightness 1-10, how specific and interconnected that group is (see calculation, page 6).
Rate its Complexity 1-10, how many variables (languages, regulations, logistics) make it harder to serve well (see calculation, page 6).
If your launch market scores low on density or high on complexity, narrow it further before spending more on acquisition.
content.explainer: Most founders launch too broadly and fail to ignite. Go narrower than feels comfortable.
questions: Is your market launch narrow enough? Should you start with one neighborhood, not the whole city?
If you tried to serve your entire target market on day one, where would the experience feel thinnest first?
What's the smallest geography or niche where you could realistically make your product feel indispensable within 90 days?

definitions.def: The problem of getting a marketplace started when neither side will join until the other side is there.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Density 8, Niche Focus 7, Tightness 7. 8 times 7 times 7 equals 392, divided by Complexity 4 equals 98. Higher means your starting market is tight and simple enough to ignite quickly. Lower means you've picked a market too broad or too complicated to get moving.
content.closing_statement: Narrower markets ignite faster than broad, thin ones.

caption:
Principle Nº 73: Cold-Start Geometry

The shape of your initial market determines your ability to start. Most founders launch too broadly and fail to ignite.

Ask yourself: Is your market launch narrow enough? Should you start with one neighborhood, not the whole city?
